transcript.text: We are just waiting on Kim as well.
Let me just check.
She's joining us.
She.
No.
That's weird.
says on her team she's free.
She shouldn't be.
She should be in this meeting.
Okay.
Anyway, for the time being, Erik, we may as well just let's just do a quick kind of round robin as to who we all are.
So, um,
And we don't need to worry, so not take up time waiting for Kim to do that so we can do that.
So, yeah, I'm Pete.
My remit essentially is kind of blogs, thought leadership, and that sort of high level kind of, yeah, high level top of funnel content falls mostly under mine.
And kind of, I plan and also upload and manage the whole blog flow.
So that's, yeah, essentially what I do.
Kieran?
Yeah, I'm Kieran.
Nice to meet you.
So I look after organic social media channels.
I think that's me, really.
It always sounds so much less than what I actually do, but when you break it down to parts, there's a lot more to that.
But it's just, you know, I look after the social media.
Thank you.
Kerry?
Hi, I'm Kerri.
I manage the campaigns team here at Enable.
So I work with everyone on this call pretty cross-functionally to make sure we can drive demand.
Okay.
And then Bax, I'll throw it to you.
You're on mute.
You're on mute, Bax.
Bax, you're on mute.
You know, the team's roulette of, like, when it throws you on mute and doesn't, when you signed in.
Sorry.
I was just saying, hi, I'm the brand creative director, so I manage the creative department and then work on brand and strategy and all that good stuff with Kim.
And finally, Kim.
Yeah, that was my pass to you, Kim.
Thank you.
Good pass.
Good pass.
I head up the corporate comms and brand team from a thought leadership standpoint, and, yeah, we're excited to talk to you about how you might be able to partner with us on some content.
So
Nice to meet you.
Absolutely.
And then I'll just quickly introduce myself, Erik.
I am an account strategist at GrowthX.
And for context, we are engaged with the enabled team to kind of build out more of their blog content.
So what we do is really help companies turn into publishers.
So we'll be aiming to push out about five blog articles per week once we get up through calibration, which is our first eight weeks.
Working with the team.
So really excited to meet you guys.
I think some of the stuff that we've been doing in the background may have made it way over.
But if not, we'd love to kind of get your guys' eyes on our writing guidelines, which is kind of the voice and tone of the brand.
So if you guys haven't seen that yet, I'll make sure to send it over.
But overall, excited to meet you guys and to chat today.
Cool.
Yeah, no, we've seen.
I think most of us have kind of input certainly stuff towards the writing guidelines.
I think you should have seen the AI style guides that I sent over via Nick.
I think that's probably the most kind of comprehensive thing in terms of our writing guidelines.
Obviously, as Bax and Kim will probably be able to kind of explain more, our brand is changing, so our tone of voice and our target audience is going to be changing slightly.
But we haven't as yet updated those style guides to take that into account.
So I don't know if there's anything else from that that you particularly wanted to highlight that we need to be kind of feeding back into.
No, I don't think so.
We did get the AI style guides, so we can definitely do another pass on the writing guidelines to make sure that we kind of incorporate all of those from you.
There we go.
I think, and you mentioned Pete, you are the one that does all the review and publishing for blog content.
So we won't, typically we do publish on behalf of our clients, but I don't think in this case we will be.
So most of the content that we create will be shared via email and then kind of reviewed, analyzed, given feedback to us.
We'll kind of update the content to make sure we incorporate all that feedback, and then we will just send the final version over to you guys for publishing onto the blog.
Cool.
So I think that's the only process we need.
Yeah, yeah, I think, sorry, Kim, go on, fire away.
No, I'm in the queue.
You go first.
No, no, no, it's fine.
You're probably going to say roughly what I was going to say anyway, but go for it.
Well, was just wondering how you're choosing what topics you're covering, because we typically pull, you know, based on things like campaigns.
They're coming up, nerd, head nerd, know, thought leadership content, things that support and push out the podcast and other thought leadership.
So the way I understand it is yours is more based on intent signals, and you're trying to capture that so that you get that SEO goodness.
Is that kind of, did I explain that right?
Yeah, I think that's a short way of saying it.
Basically, we look at kind of the broad landscape of, you know, where does enable play, where are competitors playing?
And then we kind of look for keywords that match audience intent.
And so we've done a bunch of research into kind of just what competitors are posting against and kind of gaps that you guys have.
And so we came up with target keywords, and then those target keywords are turned into what we call assignments or kind of blog articles at the highest level.
And then our team takes those kind of assignment topics, and then we create the blog content aligned to those.
Mm-hmm.

I think there was slight confusion over here, I think, over on our side in terms of I think we suggested you were going to kind of like start getting into more thought leadership kind of topics and also potentially producing larger assets.
So not just blogs, but you can also get into producing kind of guides, white papers, e-books, that kind of stuff as well.
Is that where we see it, do we see it going that route as well?

So we definitely are going to kind of index more on the editorial blog content, but we have been discussing with the team kind of around those gated assets of, you the white papers or kind of longer form content.
And so definitely stuff that we were able to support.
I think it does take a little bit longer for us to generate those and make sure that they're kind of on voice and tone, have the right depth, and meet kind of, again.
Audience intent of like what's interesting to the audience at this time.
And then we also have to do the kind of creative design and assets for those as well.
So those will be a little bit longer for us to develop.
So it's during this first eight weeks, it's really calibrating on voice, tone, are we matching technical depth and aligned with, again, the audiences.

But once we get out of this kind of eight-week strategy sprint, then we're able to kind of better support the longer form content, knowing that we know, you know, the shorter form, for lack of a better phrase, is kind of technically sound and resonates with the audience.
So we will be able to get into that longer form kind of branded, gated assets once we get out of this kind of strategy sprint.
Right.
I mean, I think so.
That's one of the key reasons for kind of getting this group together was if we do go down that more, as I say, thought leadership, more longer form content, this is the team that...
would really need to be embedded in that process because, obviously, I think, you know, one of the things we don't want to be doing is producing content for the sake of producing content.
Everything we produce needs to be usable and built into our campaigns process and all that kind of stuff.

So I think, as I say, yeah, one of the reasons for getting this together was really kind of to understand this process and then to let you know that there is a kind of very kind of strict process from our side in terms of how we produce this.
So, Kim, do you want to add anything more to that?

No, I would just say we would want to work with you guys from a planning standpoint, too, to identify, and I'm sure that you have this in mind, too, Eric, to identify what things we should be writing on because we invest, we've already invested quite a bit in some pretty big assets for Top of Funnel.
That are really geared towards driving leads and that are aligned with what Kerri is going to be putting out in campaigns.
So we're very thoughtful in our planning process about how these things come together, including events, right?
What events are we going to be at and what do we want to put out?

So maybe when we get, my suggestion would be is when we get past this eight weeks, eight week or so sprint, that maybe we can come back and the team can identify maybe some gaps where maybe we even have some, like, I would say maybe mid-funnel content needs where that intersection happens, right?
Where we kind of, we tend to have gaps there a little bit where it's like, does the product team own that or do we own that?
Who owns that?
So I think that's an area where we feel a little bit, if I were to say a little bit of pain point sometime, Kerri, Pete, feel free to jump in.
But that's where I think maybe we could probably use the most help.
Yep.
Yeah, I definitely agree with that.
Kind of understanding your guys' kind of campaign cadence and what.
Content would best help support that.

Like, the sooner we can get in, that cadence would be super critical just so we can make sure that the content we develop is both aligned with the campaign and kind of can go through your technical review cycle.
So I would say we don't have to wait until we're completely out of the eight-week strategy sprint to start gliding on that.
But I think we probably wouldn't start generating it post the eight weeks, but I think the more coordination we can do ahead of time would be the better.
And from a thematic standpoint, we already have those nailed down.
So I can put it in the chat of this meeting, just a slide that shows it, but we could also have another meeting if we need to go a bit more in depth as to what these things mean.
Absolutely.
And just while we're sort of like on that backs, obviously from...
From a creative perspective, are you comfortable with GrowthX kind of taking on a creative kind of production role with assets, or how do you see that working?
Well, I don't know.
How would that work?
Yeah.
Yep.
So we have a designer that would be working kind of based off your style guides and brand assets to kind of match the intent of the piece.

So right now, with just the editorial content, we kind of look at your blog and would love to have any other brand assets you can include, but let's look at kind of the overall colors and palette and, for lack of a better phrase, like themes of what the header images are.
And we run a AI pipeline to generate those images.
We would probably do something a little bit more hand.
created to match any of these more longer form content.
So we would definitely have a little bit more of your involvement in that process than we do with just the kind of featured header images for the blog posts that we do today.

So are we, I know we're talking blog posts and stuff like that, but are we talking longer like white paper things when we're seeing thought leadership or are these like, is that kind of what we're talking about?
So I don't think we've landed specifically on what it would be.
know we've been speaking with Nick and Andrew as we get going in these first few weeks of like the desire to have longer form content, but we haven't really landed on what that might be.
I know white papers get a little bit out of our, you know, technical feasibility range, given that these are mostly AI generated content pieces.
We do have have-
We're editors and content managers that are in the loop kind of guiding the content as it's being developed.
But as far as kind of what's the output that we want to go for aligned to the campaigns that you guys are running, we have not really aligned on what that final piece would be.
Okay.
Okay.

So the only reason I ask is, so we're kind of mid-flight on developing a little bit more of a standardized look for the brand and some of that's coloring and patterns and stuff like that, locking down.
then we do have some, like, the reason I ask is a longer format, I think, is we've got, like, report styling and the way we, like, data and stuff like that.
So we could, I think we could probably package up some stuff for you.
There might be a pivot down the road in the future on a color or something.
But right now, I think we can, we're all working toward just getting it up and running.
So we can definitely package that up and then...

Yeah, think it would, the only thing it would maybe make sense is, like, if you guys are doing content and stuff like that, right, and Pete fit me to jump in, but, like, if we took a look at, you the first couple assets that are coming down, making sure everything's feeling good, and from there, you know, it could be out for the races kind of a thing, so.
Yeah, I would certainly say that would be, in terms of producing the blog headers, I think it would be good to kind of get those, just make sure we're on point with those as we get started.
Anything else to add on that front, Bax?
Nope.
Okay, cool, so I'll, I'll, I'll, I'll liaise with Bax and ensure we can kind of, like, get some sort of, some sort of creative asset package to you, and so that will give you guidelines on that.
But now, obviously, that'll be beyond the blog headers, which you can already see.
So, you talk about, so you mentioned, obviously, this is, this, this, this.
was all kind of AI, mostly AI generated.

And with, is there any, have you discussed at all anything along the lines of technical oversight from people at our end and also whether the content that you will be producing, whether we're seeing that from your side as being specifically attributed to technical bylines over here or whether we're just, we're just expecting that to come under a sort of generic kind of enable banner for the blogs?
Yeah, I don't think we've spoken specifically about any technical reviews.
Right now, we're mostly relying on the core team that we're working with to review and kind of point out anything that might be slightly off or even unfactful.
We do have a kind of in-depth pipeline that does.
You search and then does a fact checker towards the end of it.

But I think as we get these few first pieces out, having maybe a broader team to look for any of those technical inaccuracies or maybe just if there's certain ways that you guys want to frame verbiage around that enables products, it might be worthwhile to kind of have that additional review cycle.
Especially as we get calibrated, just so we can review, you know, as we update writing guidelines and any other pieces of the pipeline on our end, just to make sure we're capturing that feedback.
Yeah.
Yeah, I would say definitely as we move forward, I think that would be good.

I mean, I do like to try and get things, I do like to try and kind of have specific bylines for things because I like to try and avoid things being too generic and having just kind of generic kind of enable bylines on blogs.

So I think if we're going to do that, then it's going to, just so you're aware that this will build into our approval and publishing process that will obviously need some sort of oversight if we're going to stick people's names on it.
And also there will be areas, certainly anything that's touching on compliance and security, potentially we may need kind of a legal review at this end as well.
Kim, don't know if you've got, in terms of legal reviews on these things, do we have a, do we have a standpoint?
We would put it more up front like we normally do, it requires legal review, compliance review, and we'll be able to tell you, and there's nuances, right?
Like sometimes if it's based on, you know, repurposing things, but then we're putting a little bit of a different spin on it, that's one thing.
Give it, give it.
It's making claims like we will make you more secure or we will do this or in Central will do this.
You know, that's where it gets tricky.
We kind of know what to avoid and we can help guide that with you and make a decision whether it's something legal needs to review.
Typically, if it's really product centric, they don't have to review it.
But like I said, if it starts to get into, you know, what I would call security trends and things like that, we're definitely going to want to have some oversight and input into what we're doing.
We have some conversations happening right now.
And one thing would probably be good for you to know, Erik, is that we're building our Threat and Tell team.

So as, you know, as a company that has a SOC and a Threat Intelligence team, we are aiming to, by the beginning of next year, have the Threat and Tell team posting regular intelligence, probably a corresponding blog, social media post that will really guide, help guide.
All right.
discussions around key trends that are happening.

Like, let's say that there's a big, you know, threat actor in the wild that everyone's talking about, or a big outage, like even we saw with AWS, or ways that we want to talk about business continuity.
And we're being very thoughtful about that.
We're building a new team.
Our CISO is building a new team to support that, because right now it's just all about the bench.
We don't have the bench to get all of that done.
But we are going to be doing more rapid response types of activities to try to both get into those new cycles and also shape those new cycles.
Those can help with SEO, but they're really more about that leadership.
So we always have to kind of balance the brand awareness with the SEO.
But I would want to make sure from our standpoint that anything you guys are going to do on security trends, we really have a little more extra oversight on.
But to make sure that they're aligned with what we're trying to, what we're trying to accomplish.
There's some, you know, some things that we have to avoid, like nation-state in general, because we don't want to put a target on our back and things like that.
So just would want to make sure we understand sort of your thoughts on that kind of content as well.
Yeah, so typically the content we produce won't be anywhere close to that kind of incident response or getting into too much thought leadership.
It is very much kind of, you know, what is cyber resilience or ransomware recovery step-by-step playbook.
Gotcha.

So some of those things are just how do we get the top and middle funnel of kind of where people are searching so that you guys show up in SEO and then eventually we get into producing enough content so that the LLMs and kind of generative engine optimization, you guys are showing up in those AI answers.
So that's really the goal of this partnership is to kind of make sure you guys are showing up when and how you want to show

Especially as more and more kind of AI answers start to pull you in of like, are we stating that, you know, enables a cyber resilience company, not specifically just piecemeal about the products, but you guys cover the whole kind of spectrum of, you know, cybersecurity and resiliency.
Okay.
That makes sense.
Kieran, did you, did you intentionally raise your hand?
I was, I was, about to ask about the, the generative engine optimization, because you'd been so quite, you'd obviously like quite focused on the SEO with it.
I was just, and I was going to ask about GEO, but then you said that just as I was about to put my hand up, so I pulled it back down again.
So I'm, I'm quite happy.
Wonderful.
Is GEO the same thing as AEO?
Yeah.
Yeah.
It's different.
I keep hearing these new terms and I'm like, what is that?
Okay.
Yeah, we, we tend to stick with GEO, but answer engine optimization, AI answers, AI overviews.
All kind of in the same definition.
So, Pete and the of the team, but I haven't had a chance to fill you in on this yet, Pete.
Our agency, our new agency in the U.S., RH Strategic, we told them we were having a call with you because they're working on predictions for 2026.
And, you know, that's one of those things that, you know, everyone's trying to talk about the same things.
And it's a very tough competition.
So, they suggested, instead of a press release, they're going to pitch out, like, there'll be predictions from all of our top leaders.
They also suggested a blog that would just kind of summarize.
And I know we've done something similar before, but maybe something like that is where, Erik, maybe you could come in and help us optimize it if they gave you, like, the outline.
Does that make sense?
And, of course, you would be involved in reviewing it.
But are there opportunities for blogs?
any that we already have in our pipeline for you to reveal an option?
So we haven't done that traditionally.

We do refreshes of, like, we look at kind of content that's on your blog that may be, you know, six months, a year, two years old, and see which ones are, like, losing clicks and which ones we may be able to refresh with new keywords so that they kind of start to re-rank.
But as far as ones that are in progress, I do not think we've done that traditionally.
Okay.
I'm not saying that we can't, but it would be a little bit new motion for us to kind of weave in with existing teams.
I think the agency can do it.
They have someone on staff, too.
So for that, we can just kind of leave that as a separate thing for now for anything we have in the pipe already.
And, Pete, we can work with you on that one, obviously.
But it sounds good.
Okay.
Okay.
Well,
We're kind of approaching time, and Kieran, Kerri, Bax, anything more to kind of, you'd like to get out of, Erik, at this stage?
I think for me, I'll probably just have to have a look at some of the stuff that you bring out, really quite a lot of stuff I make for social, as just repurposed from our blogs.

So, when I start to see sort of like the blogs that you guys are sending over, I'll be able to sort of get a better idea of what you're producing, and what I can really turn around from that, really on my end, I think.

For me, it's just being involved in the planning up front, which you said will probably take place after this eight-week or near the end of the eight-week period, and just making sure, like, I understand the value of SEO and people organically searching, but are there any places we can amplify some of these materials in our campaigns, and just making sure those things are working together?
So, that's where I'll be interested in coming in.
Thank you.
Cool.
right.
Facts, anything?
No.
Okay.
Well, Erik, it's been great to get an opportunity to meet you and kind of actually get to the bottom of what it is you guys are planning to do.

I don't think, I mean, just from my perspective in terms of just the whole kind of process and publishing process and stuff like that, obviously if it's going through, if it's going through me, there is, you know, there is, there is a little bit of, there will be a little bit of lag, although if you're doing the graphics, that's great.
And there may be, will you be doing an SEO run on this as well in terms of, in terms of adding, in terms of adding internal links and stuff like that?
Yep.
So part of our process is to add internal links for each article.
And so we'll at least have...
Internal links there.

If you guys have feedback or guidance on which internal links would be more applicable or if there's any that are just missing completely that you think would be helpful, then we're more than open to that feedback.
Okay.
Yeah, well, that will go through.
There's obviously Elaine over this side who kind of manages that particular side of the SEO process.
That will be down to her.
don't know if you've met Elaine yet.
Elaine and Andrew Proft, if you've met those two.
Yeah, they do work with them on a weekly basis.
Cool.
Okay, great.
So, yeah.
Okay, so you know all that.
That's great.
Excellent.
I don't think it's anything more to kind of discuss at the moment.
But, as I say, good to meet you.
Good to kind of get an understanding of what's going on.
We'll get – I'll lean on backs for a brand asset pack.
And, yeah, if you've – anything else, just kind of reach out.
But I look forward to kind of getting this working.
And kind of working closer with you.
Absolutely.
Great to meet everyone.
And I'm sure we'll be in touch soon.
Cool.
Great.
Erik.
Take care.
Bye.
